title: Introduction to Insolvency Law
content:
Session 1: Introduction to IBC 
Madhusudan Godhandas v Madhu Woollen Industries [1972 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Aps are a partnership firm (three Katakia brothers) running Madhu Wool Spinning Mills. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Resp had paid-up capital of Rs 5,51,000 (1,103 shares). Katakias held 3 shares; the remaining 1,100 were with Bombay 
Traders (N.C. Shah + Nandkishore & Valia groups). 
‚ùñ‚Äã In May 1965, before incorporation, Bombay Traders agreed with the appellants that: 
‚û¢‚Äã Appellants would supply and erect imported machinery for a new shoddy wool plant. 
‚û¢‚Äã Bombay Traders would advance about Rs 10 lakhs for machinery and installation, to be treated initially as a loan 
but convertible into equity. 
‚û¢‚Äã Appellants also had an option to convert their erection expenses into equity. 
‚û¢‚Äã Machinery to be installed in a shed of Ravi Industries; company to pay Rs 3,100 per month rent. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Company incorporated in July 1965 ‚Üí appellants claimed the company adopted the agreement; the company denied 
adoption. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Appellants filed a winding-up petition (Jan 1970) under Section 433(c) ‚Äì inability to pay debts, claiming the company owed 
them: 
‚û¢‚Äã Erection expenses + interest + commission (~ Rs 1.14 lakh + additions). 
‚û¢‚Äã Compensation for occupation of shed (Rs 3,100/month) + interest. 
‚û¢‚Äã Price of 3 machines (Rs 85,250) + interest + commission. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Appellants alleged the company acknowledged only Rs 72,556.01, while actual dues were much higher. 
‚û¢‚Äã The company was running deep losses (over Rs 6.21 lakhs by March 1969), stopped operations by Sept 1969, and 
was indebted to directors and related firms. 
‚û¢‚Äã Total liabilities (incl. share capital): ~Rs 14.98 lakhs; liabilities excluding share capital: ~Rs 9.56 lakhs; assets valued 
at ~Rs 8.81 lakhs; liquid assets only ~Rs 2.74 lakhs. 
‚û¢‚Äã Even proposed machinery sale at Rs 4.5 lakhs would not cover debts; appellants claimed true market value was Rs 6 
lakhs and sale was undervalued and unsanctioned. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Appellants argued: company was insolvent, substratum had disappeared, no prospects of profitable business ‚Üí just and 
equitable to wind up. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Respondent‚Äôs Position 
21 
 
‚û¢‚Äã The company disputed the debt in fact and law. 
‚û¢‚Äã Claimed no contractual relationship with the appellants regarding erection work. 
‚û¢‚Äã Asserted that the petitioners failed to prove loss of substratum. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether a winding-up order can be passed under Section 433(e) when the alleged debt is bona fide is disputed in fact or law. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the company had lost its substratum such that winding up was justified. 
‚ùñ‚Äã To what extent should the court consider the wishes of creditors and shareholders in deciding a winding-up petition. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the company‚Äôs inability to pay debts was established when debt existed itself was disputed. 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 433(e), Companies Act 1956 (Grounds for winding up) ‚Üí the company may be wound up if unable to pay its 
debts. 
‚û¢‚Äã Note: This ground originally existed in s.271 of Companies Act 2013 but was removed after the IBC 2016. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Principles for Winding Up When Debt Is Disputed ‚Üí from case law including London and Paris Banking Corporation 
and Re Tweeds Garages Ltd: 
‚û¢‚Äã If debt is bona fide disputed and the defence is substantial, no winding-up order will be passed. 
‚û¢‚Äã Inability to precisely quantify the debt is not a bar, provided the existence of the debt is clearly established. 
‚û¢‚Äã The company must show: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The defence is in good faith. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The defence is likely to succeed in law. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
There is prima facie proof of facts supporting the defence. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Existence of a Debt as Prerequisite ‚Üí Winding-up proceedings cannot commence unless the affirmative existence of a debt 
is established. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Loss of Substratum ‚Üí deemed lost when: [required to wind up a company] 
‚û¢‚Äã Objects of the company have substantially failed, or 
‚û¢‚Äã Business can be carried on only at a loss, or 
‚û¢‚Äã Liabilities far exceed assets. 
‚û¢‚Äã However: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Loss must be clearly proven. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Mere losses or sale of some assets do not prove loss of substratum unless future profitability is impossible. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Creditors‚Äô and Shareholders‚Äô Wishes (s.557 Companies Act) 
‚û¢‚Äã The court considers the wishes of creditors and shareholders and creditors‚Äô wishes normally prevail, but if majority 
creditors oppose winding up, the court may decline it. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Judicial Role Pre-IBC 
‚û¢‚Äã Courts exercised wide managerial discretion. 
‚û¢‚Äã The court evaluated business viability ‚Üí now the role of the CoC under IBC. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Dispute of Debt 
‚û¢‚Äã The company denied the existence of any debt for erection work. 
‚û¢‚Äã Produced prima facie evidence showing no contract or liability. 
‚û¢‚Äã The dispute was bona fide and substantial, not a tactic to hide inability to pay. 
‚û¢‚Äã Therefore, the existence of debt itself was not established, barring winding-up under s.433(e). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Quantification Irrelevant Where Debt Itself Is in Doubt 
‚û¢‚Äã Although law says the exact amount need not be proven if existence is clear, here the existence was disputed. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Loss of Substratum Not Proved 
‚û¢‚Äã Petitioners claimed the company ceased functioning and sold machinery. 
‚û¢‚Äã Court held: 
22 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The company had incurred some losses, but not enough to prove substratum had disappeared. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
No proof that the company abandoned its objects. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The court was reluctant to conclude business would never be profitable. 
‚û¢‚Äã Hence, no ground for winding up on this basis. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Wishes of Creditors 
‚û¢‚Äã Majority creditors did not push for winding up. 
‚û¢‚Äã Under s.557, the court considers such wishes ‚Üí here they weighed against winding up. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Pre-IBC Context 
‚û¢‚Äã The court examined profitability, business prospects, and viability‚Äîroles now shifted to CoC under IBC. 
‚û¢‚Äã Illustrates judicial overreach in insolvency matters prior to IBC. 
 
Conclusion 
The Supreme Court dismissed the winding-up petition. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Debt was bona fide disputed in fact and law. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Appellants failed to prove existence of debt, a prerequisite for s.433(e). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Substratum not lost; mere losses insufficient. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Creditors' and shareholders' interests weighed against winding up.‚Äã
 
Fertilizer Corp of India Ltd v Coromandal Sacks Private Limited  (2024 | SC | 2 J) 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Fertilizer Corporation of India Ltd. (FCIL) ‚Äì Debtor / Sick Industrial Company & Coromandal Sacks Pvt. Ltd. (CSPL) ‚Äì 
Creditor / Operational Creditor (in IBC terms) 
‚ùñ‚Äã CSPL manufactured and supplied High Density Polyethylene (HDPE) bags to FCIL under a contract. 
‚ùñ‚Äã CSPL supplied a substantial quantity of bags over and above the original purchase order, based on FCIL‚Äôs assurance that a 
subsequent purchase order would formalize and cover the excess. 
‚ùñ‚Äã When FCIL issued the next purchase order, it unilaterally reduced the price per bag. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Further grievances: 
‚û¢‚Äã FCIL refused to accept 25,000 bags, alleging poor quality. 
‚û¢‚Äã FCIL alleged delays in supply and deducted liquidated damages (LD) from the payable amount. 
‚ùñ‚Äã FCIL ultimately decided not to pay (in 1990), raising questions regarding interest accrual and the creditor‚Äìdebtor dynamic. 
‚ùñ‚Äã CSPL filed a civil suit for recovery, and won in the lower court. 
‚ùñ‚Äã On appeal, FCIL argued before the Supreme Court that the recovery suit was barred under Section 22(1) of the Sick 
Industrial Companies (Special Provisions) Act, 1985 (SICA) since it was a sick company under BIFR jurisdiction. 
 
Issue: Whether a money recovery suit filed by a creditor against a company declared as ‚Äúsick‚Äù under the SICA, 1985, is barred by 
Section 22(1) of the Act? 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Sick Industrial Companies (Special Provisions) Act, 1985 
‚û¢‚Äã Preamble: The Act was enacted ‚Äúto secure timely detection of sick and potentially sick companies‚Ä¶ and to provide 
for preventive, ameliorative, remedial and other measures for their revival.‚Äù 
‚û¢‚Äã Section 22(1): Provides a statutory moratorium ‚Äì a suspension of certain proceedings against a company once 
declared ‚Äúsick‚Äù by the BIFR, subject to specific conditions. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Winding up of the company. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Execution, distress or the like against the company‚Äôs property. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Appointment of receiver over company‚Äôs property. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Suit for recovery of money from the company. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Enforcement of any security against the company. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Enforcement of a guarantee in respect of any loan/advance made to the company. 
23 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Conditions for the Bar under Section 22(1) ‚Äì for the moratorium to apply: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
An inquiry under Section 16 is pending, or 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
A scheme under Section 17 is under preparation or consideration, or 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
A sanctioned scheme is under implementation, or 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
An appeal under Section 25 is pending. 
 
Application:  
‚ùñ‚Äã FCIL‚Äôs Status as a Sick Company 
‚û¢‚Äã At the end of FY 1991‚Äì92, FCIL‚Äôs net worth had eroded drastically. 
‚û¢‚Äã FCIL‚Äôs Board of Directors passed a resolution declaring the company ‚Äúsick‚Äù and made a reference to the BIFR 
under Section 15. 
‚û¢‚Äã The BIFR, after hearing stakeholders, declared FCIL a sick industrial company under Section 3(1)(o) on 
06.11.1992. 
‚û¢‚Äã The BIFR allowed time until 31.03.1993 for FCIL and the Government of India to submit a rehabilitation plan. 
‚û¢‚Äã The company continued to remain sick until 27.06.2013, when the BIFR finally deregistered it after concluding 
revival efforts. 
‚û¢‚Äã ‚Üí Thus, the company remained under BIFR supervision for 28 years. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Maintainability of the Recovery Suit 
‚û¢‚Äã CSPL filed the civil suit in 1996, while FCIL was still a sick company under BIFR ‚Üí therefore, Section 22(1) 
squarely applied. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The nature of the proceeding ‚Äî a money recovery suit ‚Äî falls directly within the categories specified 
under s.22(1). 
‚û¢‚Äã The pendency of BIFR proceedings meant that no civil court could entertain such recovery proceedings without 
the BIFR‚Äôs consent. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Supreme Court‚Äôs Interpretation 
‚û¢‚Äã Distinction drawn by SC between the ability to file a case and the ability to execute a decree ‚Üí filing itself could be 
barred if it interfered with the company‚Äôs revival scheme. 
‚û¢‚Äã However, the text of Section 22(1) does not explicitly distinguish between the two; hence, this interpretive move is 
contestable. 
‚û¢‚Äã Purpose of Section 22(1): 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
To suspend recovery or enforcement actions that could jeopardize rehabilitation efforts. 
‚û¢‚Äã It aims to protect the assets of the sick company from piecemeal depletion through multiple creditor actions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Mischief Rule Applied: 
‚û¢‚Äã The mischief that Parliament sought to remedy was the vacuum in law regarding protection of sick industrial units 
during rehabilitation. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The ‚Äúremedy‚Äù: Section 22(1) imposed a temporary moratorium to prevent any legal action that could 
impair revival. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The ‚Äúreason‚Äù: To ensure the revival scheme could proceed without disruption. 
‚û¢‚Äã Therefore, the civil suit for recovery of money filed by CSPL would negatively affect the assets and revival prospects 
of FCIL, thus barred under Section 22(1). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Moratorium Context (Broader Doctrinal Notes) 
‚û¢‚Äã Mokal on Moratoriums: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
A moratorium prevents individual creditors from pursuing claims against a bankrupt or sick debtor. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
It is one of the oldest principles in insolvency law, dating back to 1543. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
It ensures a collective, rather than individual, pursuit of remedies, allowing for equitable restructuring or 
revival. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Application Here: 
‚û¢‚Äã The SICA moratorium reflects this ancient policy: individual recovery is suspended to allow a collective revival 
process under BIFR supervision. 
24 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Hence, no interest accrual or individual enforcement can proceed during the moratorium ‚Äî in this case, spanning 
over two decades. 
 
Conclusion: The civil suit for recovery of money filed by CSPL was barred under Section 22(1) of the SICA, 1985, since FCIL was a 
sick company and BIFR proceedings were pending at the time of filing. 
 
Innoventive Industries v ICICI Bank [2017 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: II was the corporate debtor which failed to pay back the dues given by a group of 19 banks (headed by Central Bank) ‚Äì Banks 
filed an application for resolution process to be set in motion ‚Äì II argued that there was no debt legally due inasmuch as vide two 
Notifications dated 22-7-2015 and 18-7-2016, both under the Maharashtra Relief Undertakings (Special Provisions) Act, 1958 (the 
Maharashtra Act), all liabilities of the appellant, except certain liabilities and remedies for enforcement thereof were temporarily 
suspended [basically, a moratorium]  
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether directors of a company, who are no longer in management after appointment of an IRP, can maintain an appeal on 
behalf of the company? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the Maharashtra Relief Undertakings (Special Provisions) Act, 1958 is repugnant to the Insolvency and 
Bankruptcy Code, 2016? 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether initiation of CIRP was valid despite the moratorium under the Maharashtra Act? 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The Court relied on the shift under the IBC from debtor-in-possession (as under SICA) to creditor-in-control, emphasizing 
that control of a defaulting firm is not an inherent right of promoters.  
‚û¢‚Äã Objectives ‚Äì Jaitley Speech 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
 
‚û¢‚Äã Commercial decisions must vest in the CoC, not the state or courts.  
‚û¢‚Äã Timely resolution is essential to preserve enterprise value, and parallel moratoria cannot stall the insolvency 
process.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Issue 1: Maintainability of appeal by erstwhile directors 
‚û¢‚Äã Once IRP is appointed, management shifts from debtor promoters/directors to the insolvency professional. 
‚û¢‚Äã The directors no longer represent the company and therefore cannot file an appeal on its behalf. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Here, the company was the sole appellant, represented by persons no longer authorized to act for it. 
‚óè‚Äã
SO ‚Üí appeal held not maintainable and dismissed. 
‚û¢‚Äã SC still delivered a detailed ruling because this was the first IBC case (character of a precedent-setting decision). 
‚ùñ‚Äã Issue 2 ‚Äì Conflict between MRUA and IBC 
‚û¢‚Äã There is a direct clash ‚Üí the MRUA allows the state to take over management and impose a discretionary and 
selective moratorium, whereas the IBC mandates an automatic, comprehensive moratorium (s.14) and requires 
management to vest in the IRP/CoC, not the state.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
If the MRUA were applied, it would obstruct CIRP by preventing (i) IRP takeover, (ii) creditor control, 
and (iii) the mandatory moratorium scheme under the IBC. 
‚û¢‚Äã Under Article 254(1), the later Central law (IBC) prevails, making the conflicting part of the state law void to the 
extent of inconsistency.  
25 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Additionally, Section 238 of the IBC reinforces this by giving the Code overriding effect. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Issue 3 ‚Äì Can state moratorium block CIRP? 
‚û¢‚Äã No ‚Üí the IBC moratorium is compulsory once CIRP is admitted, while the MRUA moratorium is optional, 
uncertain, and debtor-protective.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Allowing the state moratorium to continue would defeat the creditor-in-control model and the 
time-bound insolvency process.  
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Since default existed, Section 6 was validly triggered, and CIRP initiation was lawful. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The appeal by former directors was not maintainable. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The MRUA is repugnant to the IBC and inoperative to the extent of conflict. 
‚û¢‚Äã The state moratorium cannot block CIRP; initiation of CIRP by ICICI was valid. 
‚û¢‚Äã The IBC prevails due to Article 254(1) and Section 238, affirming creditor control and IRP authority upon 
default.
 
26

Madhusudan Godhandas v Madhu Woollen Industries
Fertilizer Corp of India Ltd v Coromandal Sacks Private Limited
